summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Persistent iron deficiency anemia and related symptoms.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports a long-standing history of iron deficiency anemia, dating back to age 13. He was unable to donate blood around December and subsequently received notification of anemia. He has experienced fatigue, feverishness with chills, headaches, and occasional wheezing with activity. He notes chilling sensations and increased sensitivity to cold. He reports worsening anxiety and depression. His last colonoscopy was approximately five to six years ago and revealed no polyps. He is currently taking ferrous sulfate 25mg twice daily with orange juice and vitamin B12. He denies any history of sedation issues.

Findings
Physical exam reveals no significant findings. The patient reports no tenderness to palpation of the abdomen.

Assessment
The patient's symptoms and history are consistent with persistent iron deficiency anemia, complicated by possible underlying gastrointestinal pathology. Further investigation is warranted.

Plan
We will schedule an upper endoscopy and colonoscopy to evaluate for sources of blood loss. A referral to a hematologist has been sent. We will also initiate iron supplementation with ferrous sulfate, recommending concurrent vitamin C intake to enhance absorption. Follow-up in four weeks is scheduled to assess response to treatment.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Iron deficiency anemia.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Christina Cooper is a 65-year-old female who presents with a long-history of iron deficiency anemia.

The patient reports she has been living with anemia since she was 13 years old. She attempted to donate blood on 12/2019 but was unable to due to her anemia. She has a history of heavy menstruation and a hysterectomy in 1996. She completed a colonoscopy 5-6 years ago with nonsignificant findings. She denies having a pacemaker, defibrillator, sleep apnea, or using oxygen.

Mrs. Cooper reports a myriad of symptoms, stating she feels fatigued often during the day, feverish with chills at times, and during activity feels she cannot catch her breath and some wheezing is present. She has been having some headaches which is uncommon for her. Additionally, she is noticing tingling sensations, cold sensitivity, and her anxiety and depression have worsened.

PAST HISTORY

Surgical
Hysterectomy, 1996.

Procedures
Colonoscopy 5-6 years ago.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Drink’s alcohol 1-2 times per year.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Ferrous Sulfate 25 mg tablet twice per day (1 in the morning, 1 in the evening), Vitamin B12 OTC.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
Abdomen non-tender.

ASSESSMENT

• Iron deficiency anemia

Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

PLAN

I have asked her to continue each ferrous sulfate dose with vitamin C found in orange juice for the next 12 weeks then recheck CBC, iron ferritin, B-12, and folate. A referral was sent for her to see a hematologist. We will plan for EGD and colonoscopy to assess for potential sources of anemia or GI bleed. If this is inconclusive, capsule endoscopy can be considered.

INSTRUCTIONS

Schedule upper endoscopy, colonoscopy and appointment with hematologist. Return to the clinic if symptoms worsen.

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Persistent iron deficiency anemia and related symptoms.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports a long-standing history of iron deficiency anemia, dating back to age 13. He was unable to donate blood around December and subsequently received notification of anemia. section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports no tenderness to palpation of the abdomen.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is phrased as a patient-reported symptom/absence of symptom rather than an examiner-observed finding. However, it does not substantially mislead the reader, as it still conveys lack of abdominal tenderness.

section_miscategorizations.text: He is currently taking ferrous sulfate 25mg twice daily with orange juice and vitamin B12.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Current medication state is often documented in a separate meds or objective section; per the provided semantics, it fits better under Findings than HPI. Its placement in HPI is common in practice and not seriously misleading, so this is a minor issue.

section_miscategorizations.text: We will also initiate iron supplementation with ferrous sulfate, recommending concurrent vitamin C intake to enhance absorption.
section_miscategorizations.reason: There is a mild internal inconsistency: the HPI states the patient is already taking ferrous sulfate, while the Plan states it will be initiated. This is more a content inconsistency than a section misplacement, but could cause some confusion about whether this is a new or ongoing medication.
